perturbation.original_text: Company shall ensure that all uses of the name of the NFLA comply with applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall endeavor to ensure that uses of the name of the NFLA generally align with applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall ensure' to 'shall endeavor to ensure' and 'comply' to 'generally align' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, making compliance optional rather than mandatory. This potentially contradicts California's consumer protection laws by suggesting a lower standard of compliance than legally required.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with applicable laws, which could lead to misleading advertising or promotion. Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising, and the ambiguous language could be interpreted as allowing the company to make claims that are not fully compliant with the law, thus violating consumer protection regulations. By only requiring 'general alignment', the endorsement deal allows Gridiron to skirt the full expectations of the law.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall ensure' to 'shall endeavor to ensure' significantly weakens the obligation to comply with applicable laws. The original text mandates compliance, whereas the modified text only requires an attempt to align, creating ambiguity and potentially leading to non-compliance. Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17500, as shown in the provided snippet, states that it is unlawful to disseminate any statement concerning services or property that could induce the public into an obligation based on false or misleading information. The modification introduces a loophole where the company may not fully comply with the law, thereby potentially misleading consumers and violating the statute's intent to prevent false advertising.

perturbation.original_text: Past due payments under this Agreement shall bear interest at the rate of: (a) 1% per month; or (b) the maximum interest rate permissible under law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due payments under this Agreement may bear interest at a reasonable rate not exceeding the maximum interest rate permissible under law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall bear interest at the rate of' to 'may bear interest at a reasonable rate not exceeding' introduces ambiguity regarding whether interest will actually be charged. The term 'reasonable rate' is vague and could be open to interpretation, leading to disputes. This potentially contradicts California usury laws, which set clear limits on interest rates.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's usury law (Cal. Const. Art. XV § 1) sets limits on interest rates for loans and forbearances. By replacing the definite interest rate (1% per month or the maximum permissible) with 'a reasonable rate not exceeding the maximum,' the agreement becomes vague, potentially allowing for arbitrarily determined interest charges that, while theoretically capped, lack specific definition and enforcement mechanisms. This ambiguity conflicts with the purpose of the usury law, which is to protect borrowers from excessive interest rates.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a definite interest rate with a 'reasonable rate not exceeding the maximum,' creating ambiguity. This ambiguity contradicts the intent of California's usury law (Cal. Const. Art. XV § 1), as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which aims to protect borrowers from excessive interest rates by setting limits. The original text provided a clear calculation (1% per month or the maximum permissible), while the modified text opens the door to subjective interpretation, undermining the law's protection.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees to protect, indemnify and hold harmless the NFLA / NFLA-NC and their authorized agents, or any of them, from and against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, including reasonable attorney's fees, arising out of, or in any way connected with, actions or omissions of Company, any advertising material furnished by, or an behalf of, Company, or any claim or action for personal injury, death or other cause of action involving alleged defects in Company's Products or services. Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, general commercial and product liability insurance.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to reasonably endeavor to protect, indemnify and hold harmless the NFLA / NFLA-NC and their authorized agents, or any of them, from and against expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs arising out of, or connected with, actions or omissions of Company, related to advertising material furnished by Company, or any action for personal injury connected with defects in Company's Products, provided that the NFLA/NFLA-NC promptly notifies Company of such issues. Company agrees to seek to provide and maintain general commercial and product liability insurance.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the indemnification and insurance obligations. Replacing 'protect, indemnify and hold harmless' with 'reasonably endeavor to protect, indemnify and hold harmless' makes the obligation less stringent. Removing 'any and all expenses' and replacing it with just 'expenses' limits the scope of indemnification. Adding a prompt notification requirement could unfairly penalize the NFLA/NFLA-NC if they fail to immediately notify the company. Changing 'agrees to provide and maintain' to 'agrees to seek to provide and maintain' introduces ambiguity about whether insurance will actually be obtained. This potentially contradicts California law regarding product liability and the implied warranty of merchantability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1791.1 establishes an implied warranty of merchantability for consumer goods sold in California. The modified indemnification clause, by weakening the company's obligation to protect the NFLA/NFLA-NC from claims related to product defects and by making insurance coverage discretionary ('seek to provide'), reduces the protection afforded by the implied warranty. The 'prompt notification' requirement creates a condition that could allow the company to avoid liability even if its products are defective, undermining the purpose of the Song-Beverly Act to ensure that consumers are protected from defective goods. It allows the company to provide just compensation at its own discretion.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.7. CONSUMER WARRANTIES [1790 - 1797.96] ( Title 1.7 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) CHAPTER 1. Consumer Warranty Protection [1790 - 1795.8] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) ARTICLE 2. Definitions [1791 - 1791.3] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) 1791.1. As used in this chapter: (a) “Implied warranty of merchantability” or “implied warranty that goods are merchantable” means that the consumer goods meet each of the following: (1) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description. (2) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. (3) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled. (4) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label. (b) “Implied warranty of fitness” means (1) that when the retailer, distributor, or manufacturer has reason to know any particular purpose for which the consumer goods are required, and further, that the buyer is relying on the skill and judgment of the seller to select and furnish suitable goods, then there is an implied warrant
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities and weakens obligations related to product liability, potentially contradicting the implied warranty of merchantability under California law. The original text requires the company to 'protect, indemnify and hold harmless' and 'provide and maintain' insurance. The changed text weakens these obligations by using 'reasonably endeavor to protect' and 'seek to provide and maintain', making them less definitive. Cal. Civ. Code § 1791.1 defines 'implied warranty of merchantability' as goods that 'Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.' By weakening the indemnification and insurance obligations, the modified contract reduces the protection afforded by this implied warranty, because the company may not be obligated to cover costs associated with defective goods, contradicting the consumer protection intent of the law.
